sections.title: A Potter's Family
sections.content_summary: This section introduces the traditional craft of pottery through the story of Mini and her grandfather Appooppan, who makes pots from clay by wetting it, kneading it like dough, shaping it on a stone wheel, and baking it in a kiln. The whole family participates — Ammu (mother) handles the kiln firing, while Mini helps by decorating pots with patterns and colours. Common clay items introduced include pots, gullak (piggy bank), kulhad (cup), and birdbath, illustrating the everyday usefulness of potter's craft. The section highlights intergenerational knowledge transfer and the family as the unit of traditional craft production.

tasks.task_text: What are pots used for?
What other kinds of things do potters make?
Why is the pot baked in a kiln?

Visit a potter in your neighbourhood with your parents or elders in the family. Observe how the potter works on the stone wheel or the potter's wheel. Have you wondered how clay takes a beautiful shape on a spinning wheel? Which of the products made by a potter do you use at home? Draw some of the products that you have seen at the potter's place in the box given below.

Make your own clay (Do this activity with the help of elders): Collect soil from wet or muddy places. Remove any big pebbles or stones, sand, leaves or parts of plants from it using a net or sieve. Put this soil in a flat vessel filled with water. After a few days, throw away the water that stays on top. Now make dough that you can shape into a ball. Make any two things you like with the clay that you have made. Put it out in the sun to dry.

sections.title: Patterns in Nature
sections.content_summary: This section explores patterns — repeating shapes and colours — found in the natural world, such as stripes on a zebra, spots on a leopard, stripes on a squirrel, and leaf arrangements on a neem tree. It connects nature's patterns to the artistic tradition of decorating pottery, noting that many artists draw inspiration from plants and animals. Students are invited to observe, identify, and draw patterns on plants, animals, and objects, and to recognise pattern diversity in traditional Indian pottery from different regions.

tasks.task_text: Arrange the images in the correct order by numbering them from 1 to 6.
Look around for patterns on plants. Draw any pattern of a leaf in the given box. Give an example of your own.
Look around for patterns on animals. Draw an animal which has patterns on its body.
Look around for patterns on things. Draw a pattern in the box.

sections.title: Brick by Brick
sections.content_summary: This section extends the theme of clay-based making to bricks, explaining that bricks are formed from mud using moulds and baked in a kiln — the same fundamental process as pottery. The narrative follows Rohan in Jaipur watching a building rise brick by brick, building appreciation for the labour of construction workers. The section introduces traditional mud houses as an environmentally adapted building practice — using cow dung as a natural plaster that keeps homes cool and insect-free. Students explore diverse types of houses (tents, multi-storey buildings, stone houses, houseboats) and safety practices at construction sites.

tasks.task_text: Are all houses made of bricks?
What do you think are other materials used to build houses?
Why do you think mud houses are better for the environment?
Look around at the houses in your neighbourhood. What are the houses made of?
Look at the pictures below. Guess the name of each type of house. Fill it in the given table below.

Make a Wall: Make some bricks using a matchbox mould, small paper boxes, clay or atta (dough). Stack them in these two arrangements shown in the drawing. Which one is sturdier? Which one falls more easily when pushed?
Are there any safety rules at home?
Do you have any safety rules at school?

sections.title: Let us reflect
sections.content_summary: This closing section consolidates learning across the chapter through structured reflection tasks. Students write about pot-making processes, artistic inspiration, kilns, house materials, and the making of everyday objects; draw different kinds of houses; discuss reasons for housing diversity, safety gear, and nature's patterns; and conduct interviews with local craft-makers or elders to discover how common objects such as pencils, erasers, and notebooks are made.

tasks.task_text: What is the process of pot making?
How do artists get ideas for their arts?
What is a kiln?
What are houses made of?
You may also try to find videos on YouTube that show the process of things being made. Write down the steps in as much detail as possible.
Draw and colour any three different kinds of houses.
Why do we have different kinds of houses?
Why do people wear helmets at construction sites?
What would happen if there were no patterns in nature?
Interview someone who makes crafts or toys in your locality. Share the information in the class.
Find out how some commonly used objects like pencils, erasers, pens and notebooks are made. Ask your elders.

dual_strand.natural: Properties of clay and mud as natural materials that can be shaped and hardened; patterns found in nature on plants and animals
dual_strand.human_cultural: Traditional craft of pottery and brick-making passed across generations; diverse housing traditions adapted to local environments; artistic decoration of clay objects inspired by nature
